summary:
During fetal development, the tongue forms primarily through tuberculum impar, hypobranchial eminences, and linguolental swellings; however, arytenoid swellings develop into parts of the larynx rather than contributing to the tongue itself. Thus, arytenoids fit as an exception among these structures involved in forming other aspects of oral anatomy. To summarize concisely:

The tongue is formed by different swellings during early development—except where arytenoid swellings form part of the larynx instead. Arytenoids thus stand out uniquely here due their distinct anatomical function unrelated directly to tongue composition or growth pathways described elsewhere within this context involving more direct contributors like the others listed initially mentioned above which actually shape it properly including those noted earlier such as tubercular impars etc...

formatted_summary:
agent2 (Round 2 summarized): During fetal development, the tongue forms primarily through tuberculum impar, hypobranchial eminences, and linguolental swellings; however, arytenoid swellings develop into parts of the larynx rather than contributing to the tongue itself. Thus, arytenoids fit as an exception among these structures involved in forming other aspects of oral anatomy. To summarize concisely:

The tongue is formed by different swellings during early development—except where arytenoid swellings form part of the larynx instead. Arytenoids thus stand out uniquely here due their distinct anatomical function unrelated directly to tongue composition or growth pathways described elsewhere within this context involving more direct contributors like the others listed initially mentioned above which actually shape it properly including those noted earlier such as tubercular impars etc...
